Prix]] ([[Nazareth Speedway|Nazareth]]) | Champ_Wins = 22 | Champ_Podiums = 65 | Champ_Poles = 17 }} }} '''Emerson Fittipaldi''' ({{IPA|pt-br|ˈɛmeʁsõ fitʃiˈpawdʒi|lang}}; born 12 December 1946) is a Brazilian former [[racing driver]] and [[motorsport]] executive, who competed in [[Formula One]] from {{F1|1970}} to {{F1|1980}}. Fittipaldi won two [[Formula One World Drivers' Championship titles]], which he won in {{F1|1972}} and {{F1|1974}} with [[Team Lotus|Lotus]] and [[McLaren]], respectively; he won 14 [[Formula One Grands Prix|Grands Prix]] across 11 seasons. In [[American open-wheel racing]], Fittipaldi won the [[PPG Indy Car World Series|IndyCar World Series]] in [[1989 PPG Indy Car World Series|1989]] with [[Patrick Racing|Patrick]], and is a [[List of Indianapolis 500 winners|two-time winner]] of the [[Indianapolis 500]]. Moving up from [[Formula Two]], Fittipaldi made his race debut for [[Team Lotus]] as a third driver at the [[1970 British Grand Prix]]. After [[Jochen Rindt]] was killed at the [[1970 Italian Grand Prix]], the Brazilian became Lotus's lead driver in only his fifth Grand Prix. He enjoyed considerable success with Lotus, winning the [[List of Formula One World Drivers' Champions|World Drivers' Championship]] in [[1972 Formula One season|1972]] at the age of 25. At the time, he was the youngest ever F1 world champion, and he held the record for 33 years. He later moved to [[McLaren]] for [[1974 Formula One season|1974]], winning the title once again, and helping McLaren win their first [[List of Formula One World Constructors' Champions|Constructors' Championship]]. He surprised the paddock by moving to his brother's [[Fittipaldi Automotive]] team prior to the [[1976 Formula One season|1976 season]], being replaced by [[James Hunt]]. Success eluded him during his final years in Formula One, with the Fittipaldi cars not competitive enough to fight for victories. Fittipaldi took two more podium finishes, before retiring in [[1980 Formula One season|1980]]. Following his Formula One career, Fittipaldi moved to the American [[Championship Auto Racing Teams|CART]] series, achieving numerous successes, including the [[1989 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1989 CART title]] and two wins at the [[Indianapolis 500]] in [[1989 Indianapolis 500|1989]] and [[1993 Indianapolis 500|1993]]. Since his retirement from [[Indy Car]] racing in [[1996 PPG Indy Car World Series|1996]], Fittipaldi races only occasionally. In 2008, he became one of only three people in history to have a Corvette production car named in his honor. At age 67, he entered the [[2014 6 Hours of São Paulo]]. ==Early life== Emerson Fittipaldi was born on 12 December 1946 in [[São Paulo]], [[Brazil]].{{cite web|date=18 July 2020|title=Na Garagem: Fittipaldi estreia na Fórmula 1 e fecha GP da Inglaterra em 8º - Fórmula 1|url=https://www.grandepremio.com.br/f1/noticias/na-garagem-fittipaldi-estreia-na-formula-1-e-fecha-gp-da-inglaterra-em-8o/|access-date=11 April 2022|website=Grande Prêmio}} He is the younger son of Italian-Brazilian motorsports journalist and radio commentator Wilson Fittipaldi Sr{{cite web|url=http://www.comune.trecchina.pz.it/Trecchina_Storia.aspx|title=Trecchina, un po' di storia|author=Comune di Trecchina|access-date=17 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160306010715/http://www.comune.trecchina.pz.it/Trecchina_Storia.aspx|archive-date=6 March 2016|url-status=dead}} and his wife Józefa "Juzy" Wojciechowska, an immigrant from [[Saint Petersburg]], Russia, of [[Polish people|Polish]] and [[Russians|Russian]] descent.{{Citation needed|date=December 2020}} Fittipaldi was named after American author and philosopher [[Ralph Waldo Emerson]]. Both of his parents had raced production cars shortly after [[World War II]], and his father was responsible for the first [[Mil Milhas Brasil|Mil Milhas]] race in 1956, in São Paulo, having been inspired by the 1949 [[Italy|Italian]] [[Mille Miglia]]. Emerson, along with his brother Wilson, became motorsports enthusiasts as young children.{{cite web |url= http://en.espn.co.uk/f1/motorsport/driver/926.html |title= Emerson Fittipaldi |access-date= 7 February 2018 |publisher= ESPN }} ==Career history== [[File:1969 F3 Guards Trophy Brands Hatch Emerson Fittipaldi Lotus 59.jpg|thumb|Fittipaldi driving the Jim Russell Racing Drivers School [[Formula Three|F3]] [[Lotus 59]] in the 1969 F3 Guards Trophy at [[Brands Hatch]]]] At age 14, Fittipaldi was racing [[motorcycle]]s, and at 16, [[Hydroplane (boat)|hydroplane]]s. While racing one day, his brother [[Wilson Fittipaldi Júnior|Wilson]] blew over at {{convert|70|mi/h|km/h|abbr=on}} and landed upside down. Wilson was uninjured in the accident, but it prompted both Fittipaldi brothers to stop competing in boat racing and focus solely on racing land vehicles.{{cite web |url= https://www.mclaren.com/formula1/blog/emerson-fittipaldi/motorbikes-mclaren/ |title= From motorbikes to McLaren |date= 14 March 2013 |access-date= 7 February 2018 |publisher= McLaren Racing }} In 1967, Fittipaldi won the 6 Hours of Interlagos in a [[Volkswagen Karmann Ghia]] at the age of 20, and a year later the 12 Hours of Porto Alegre. The pair moved to racing [[Formula Vee]]s, and built up a company with their parents. In his second season in single-seaters, Fittipaldi won the Brazilian Formula Vee title at age 21. He left for Europe in 1969, with the ambition to convince team owners of his talent in three months. After some podiums and his first victories in [[Formula Ford]], Fittipaldi was first trained and then subsequently engaged by the Jim Russell Driving School [[Formula Three]] team. He won nine F3 races on the Jim Russell [[Lotus 59]] in the MCD Lombard Championship to become the [[1969 British Formula Three season|1969]] champion.{{cite web |url= https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/database/drivers/emerson-fittipaldi |title= Emerson Fittipaldi |access-date= 7 February 2018 |publisher= Motor Sport Magazine }} ===Formula Two=== For {{F2|1970}}, Fittipaldi moved up to F2 by joining the Lotus semi-works Team Bardahl campaigning [[Lotus 59|Lotus 59B]]. With six finishes in the points and four on the podium, he ended the eight-race season in third place behind [[Clay Regazzoni]] and [[Derek Bell (racing driver)|Derek Bell]]. While this result was very impressive for the newcomer to the series, the spotlight was on Fittipaldi that year because of his activities in Formula One instead. ===Formula One=== ====Lotus (1970–1973)==== Based on the success of the [[Cosworth DFV]] engine and [[Lotus 49]]/[[Lotus 49B|49B]] cars in 1968, [[Team Lotus]] was enjoying the reputation as one of the top F1 teams with the inflow of sponsorship money, and [[Colin Chapman]] used the third seat on the team for championship races as the testing ground for younger drivers. This was in contrast to the team's tradition to use non-championship F1 events for the purpose. The third seat was given to [[Alex Soler-Roig]] in early 1970, and then to Fittipaldi starting with the [[1970 British Grand Prix|British GP]] in July, with [[Jochen Rindt]] and [[John Miles (auto racer)|John Miles]] as the regular seat holders. Fittipaldi scored a fourth place as the No. 3 driver at the next [[1970 German Grand Prix|German GP]] where the No. 1 Jochen Rindt won, and the No. 2 John Miles retired. [[Team Lotus]] plans for the season drastically changed when Jochen Rindt was killed at [[Autodromo Nazionale Monza|Monza]] in September and became the only driver to win the championship posthumously. John Miles also left the team, and Fittipaldi was promoted to be the Lotus No. 1 driver on his fifth F1 race at the [[1970 United States Grand Prix|United States GP]] with [[Reine Wisell]] and [[Pete Lovely]] as the teammates. Fittipaldi proved up to the task and won this first post-Rindt race for Lotus. In his first full year as Lotus's lead driver in 1971, Fittipaldi finished sixth in the Drivers' Championship as the team further developed the previous season's [[Lotus 72]]. Fittipaldi proved dominant in 1972, as he won five of 11 races and claimed the F1 Drivers' Championship. At 25, Fittipaldi was then the [[List of Formula One driver records#Youngest World Drivers' Championship winners|youngest champion]] in F1 history. It appeared he might do it again in 1973. After three wins in four attempts with the 72D, he began to struggle in the new 72E that was unveiled mid-year. It resulted in the reverse of the previous year, with [[Jackie Stewart]] beating Fittipaldi for the Drivers' Championship. ====McLaren (1974–1975)==== Fittipaldi left Lotus to sign with the promising [[Team McLaren|McLaren]] team. Driving the highly efficient [[McLaren M23]], he had three victories in 1974, reached the podium four other times, and beat out [[Clay Regazzoni]] in a close battle for his second championship. The following season, he notched two more victories and four other podiums, but was second to a dominant [[Niki Lauda]]. ====Fittipaldi (1976–1980)==== However, at the height of his F1 success, Fittipaldi shocked everyone by leaving McLaren to race for older brother [[Wilson Fittipaldi Júnior|Wilson Fittipaldi]]'s [[Copersucar]]-sponsored [[Fittipaldi Automotive]] team.{{citation needed|date=July 2017}} Fittipaldi remained with the team for five seasons but only managed a best finish of second. Fittipaldi decided to retire from racing at the end of 1980. He has since said that his last two years in Formula One were very unhappy: "I was too involved in the problems of trying to make the team work, and I neglected my marriage and my personal life",Ludvigsen, Karl (2002) [https://www.amazon.co.uk/exec/obidos/ASIN/185960837X 'Emerson Fittipaldi'] p. 136 Haynes Group. {{ISBN|1-85960-837-X}} although at the time he cited the deaths of many of his colleagues as his reason."Fittipaldi to Retire", ''The Times'', 12 December 1980, pg. 20 He was only 33, but had been racing in Formula One for a decade. He had failed to finish seven of the last ten races that year and had several times been outpaced by his Finnish teammate [[Keke Rosberg]] (a future champion himself). He moved into the management of the team{{cite web|url=https://8w.forix.com/efittipaldi.html|title=Emerson Fittipaldi|work=8w.forix.com|access-date=28 February 2006}} alongside his brother. The team struggled on for another two years with minimal sponsorship, going into receivership at the end of 1982.{{cite web|url=http://www.pressreader.com/canada/edmonton-journal/20121009/282286727494059|title=Emerson Fittipaldi|work=[[Edmonton Journal]]|date=9 October 2012|access-date=7 February 2018}} File:1971 Emerson Fittipaldi, Lotus 72 (kl).JPG|Fittipaldi driving the [[Lotus 72]] at the [[Nürburgring]] in {{F1|1971}} File:Emerson Fittipaldi 1972 Austrian GP.jpg|Fittipaldi at the wheel of the Lotus 72D at the [[1972 Austrian Grand Prix]] File:Emerson Fittipaldi GP74 01.jpg|Fittipaldi at [[Silverstone Circuit|Silverstone]] in 1974 File:Emerson Fittipaldi McLaren M23 1974 Britain.jpg|Fittipaldi in the [[McLaren M23]] in the [[1974 Race of Champions]] at [[Brands Hatch]] File:Copersucar FD04 1976.jpg|Fittipaldi driving for his brother's [[Fittipaldi Automotive|eponymous team]] at the [[1976 Brazilian Grand Prix]] at his home circuit, [[Autódromo José Carlos Pace|Interlagos]] File:A Escuderia Emerson Fittipaldi Copersucar, Jacarepagua, 1978.tif|Fittipaldi Copersucar, [[Autódromo Internacional Nelson Piquet|Jacarepaguá]], 1978 === CART === After leaving F1 in 1980, Fittipaldi took time out from major racing for four years. In 1984, the 37-year-old Fittipaldi made his debut in the American [[Championship Auto Racing Teams|CART]] series. He spent his first season acclimatising to IndyCars, driving for two teams before joining [[Patrick Racing]] as a replacement for [[Chip Ganassi]], who had been seriously injured in the 1984 [[Michigan 500]]. The 1985 Michigan 500 marked Fittipaldi's first victory in CART. Fittipaldi stayed five years with Patrick Racing, recording six victories and solid finishes in the overall standings.{{cite web|url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/database/drivers/emerson-fittipaldi|title=Emerson Fittipaldi|access-date=7 February 2018}} In 1989, Fittipaldi had five wins, finished in the top five in every race he completed, and was the CART champion. Among his wins was a dominant performance in the [[1989 Indianapolis 500]], where he led 158 of 200 laps and won by two laps, but only after a dramatic duel with [[Al Unser Jr.]] in the closing laps of the race. Unser ran down Fittipaldi after a late-race restart and passed him for the lead on lap 196. Three laps later, Fittipaldi used lapped traffic to his advantage to pull alongside Unser on the backstretch. Neither driver would give way, and the two cars touched wheels as they went through turn three side by side. Unser's car spun out of control to hit the outside wall, while Fittipaldi was able to maintain sufficient control to keep his car moving straight. In spite of the altercation, Unser applauded Fittipaldi from the infield as Fittipaldi passed by on the final lap.{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1989/05/29/sports/fittipaldi-wins-indy-500-after-collision-with-unser.html/|title=Fittipaldi Wins Indy 500 After Collision With Unser|work=[[The New York Times]]|date=29 May 1989|access-date=7 February 2018}} [[File:1990indy500fittipaldi.jpg|thumb|Fittipaldi during the [[1990 Indianapolis 500]]]] [[Roger Penske]] hired Fittipaldi for his [[Team Penske|racing team]] in 1990 and he continued to be among the top drivers in CART, winning at least one race with Penske for six straight years. But for bad luck he might have won three consecutive Indianapolis 500s, suffering blistered tires in 1990 and a gearbox failure in 1991, both while leading. In [[1993 Indianapolis 500|1993]] he added a second Indianapolis 500 victory by taking the lead from reigning Formula One World Champion [[Nigel Mansell]] on lap 185 and holding it for the remainder.{{cite web|url=http://sports.usatoday.com/2016/05/25/four-days-to-the-100th-indy-500-emerson-fittipaldi-and-the-orange-juice/|title=That's entertainment?|work=[[Motor Sport (magazine)|Motor Sport]]|date=1 July 1993|access-date=7 February 2018|archive-date=28 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190328115956/https://sports.usatoday.com/2016/05/25/four-days-to-the-100th-indy-500-emerson-fittipaldi-and-the-orange-juice/|url-status=dead}} [[File:Emerson-fittipaldi mid-ohio keyhole.jpg|thumb|250px|Fittipaldi navigating the Keyhole section at [[Mid-Ohio Sports Car Course]] in 1992]] The race saw Fittipaldi break Indianapolis victory lane tradition when he drank a celebratory bottle of orange juice before the traditional bottle of milk. He was only the second driver to not drink milk at Indianapolis since the tradition was founded in 1936 (and firmly established in 1956). Fittipaldi owned several orange groves in his native Brazil, and wanted to promote the citrus industry. Fan reaction was negative to the break in tradition despite the fact that Fittipaldi did drink milk shortly after.{{cite web|url=http://sports.usatoday.com/2016/05/25/four-days-to-the-100th-indy-500-emerson-fittipaldi-and-the-orange-juice/|title=Four days to the 100th Indianapolis 500: Emerson Fittipaldi and the Orange Juice|work=[[USA Today]]|date=25 May 2016|access-date=7 February 2018|archive-date=28 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190328115956/https://sports.usatoday.com/2016/05/25/four-days-to-the-100th-indy-500-emerson-fittipaldi-and-the-orange-juice/|url-status=dead}} As a result of drinking the juice, Fittipaldi forfeited $5,000 from the winner's purse and publicly apologized to the American Dairy Association.{{cite web|url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/1993/06/03/Auto-Racing-Notebook/4794739080000/|title=Auto Racing Notebook|work=UPI Archives|date=3 June 1993|access-date=7 February 2018}} Fan reaction to the milk snub was highly negative, and he was booed a week later at [[ABC Supply Company A.J. Foyt 225|Milwaukee]], a center of the American dairy industry. In the years that followed, many fans continued to hold the action against him. In interviews since, Fittipaldi explained his action, and apologised for the wave of negativity that followed. Fittipaldi returned to Indianapolis to drive the [[Chevrolet Corvette]] [[Pace Car]] for the [[2008 Indianapolis 500]]. Despite the passage of 15 years, he was again booed and heckled by some fans during the parade laps.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2016/oct/30/lewis-hamilton-formula-one-emerson-fittipaldi|title=Lewis Hamilton is a great example for the other F1 drivers, says Emerson Fittipaldi|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=30 October 2016|access-date=7 February 2018}} [[File:fittipaldi indy.jpg|thumb|Fittipaldi racing in the [[Indianapolis 500]] in 1994]] In May 1994, Fittipaldi skipped a practice session for the [[1994 Indianapolis 500|Indianapolis 500]] after his close friend [[Ayrton Senna]], also a native of Brazil and a former Formula One champion, [[Death of Ayrton Senna|died in a crash]]. Fittipaldi was one of the pallbearers during Senna's funeral, alongside [[Jackie Stewart]], [[Alain Prost]] and several other F1 world champions. Fittipaldi nearly won his third 500 but clipped the turn 4 wall with 15 laps to go while he was holding a nearly full lap lead over teammate Unser Jr.{{cite web|url=https://www.motorsport.com/indycar/news/1994-indianapolis-500-an-all-in-gamble-pays-off/|title=1994 Indianapolis 500: an all-in gamble pays off|date=31 January 2014|access-date=7 February 2018}} Approaching 50, Fittipaldi was still driving in [[Championship Auto Racing Teams|CART]] in 1996 when an injury at [[Michigan International Speedway]] ended his career. Fittipaldi did not return to the series as a driver after the injury. Fittipaldi finished his CART career with 22 wins. In 2003 he made a return to CART as a team owner.{{cite web|url=https://autoweek.com/article/car-news/emerson-fittipaldi-returns-cart-owner-fittipaldi-dingman-racing-team|title=Emerson Fittipaldi returns to CART as owner with Fittipaldi-Dingman Racing team|work=[[Autoweek]]|date=10 February 2003|access-date=7 February 2018}} ===Later career=== [[File:Emerson Fittipaldi SP Indy300 2011.JPG|thumb|right|Fittipaldi at the [[2011 São Paulo Indy 300]]. He waved the green flag at the start of the race. ]] Fittipaldi was the acting team principal for the Brazilian [[A1 GP]] entry.Mohapatra, Bikash (7 October 2007). [https://www.dnaindia.com/sports/report-he-is-still-a1-1125956/ "He is still A1"], ''[Daily News and Analysis]''. Retrieved on 7 September 2021. In 2005 Fittipaldi made a surprise return to competitive racing in the [[Grand Prix Masters]] event held at [[Kyalami]] in [[South Africa]], finishing second behind fellow F1 driver [[Nigel Mansell]].{{cite web|url=https://mg.co.za/article/2005-11-14-mansell-wins-sa-grand-prix-masters|title=Mansell wins SA Grand Prix Masters|work=[[Mail & Guardian]]|date=14 November 2005|access-date=7 web|url=https://globoesporte.globo.com/ESP/Noticia/Motor/0,,MUL417823-1310,00.html|title=Emerson Fittipaldi vai correr na GT3 Brasil|publisher=globo.com|access-date=30 July 2008}} In 2011, he started embracing social media and became a [[Chairman]] of Motorsport.com.{{cite web|url=http://markets.on.nytimes.com/research/stocks/news/press_release.asp?docTag=201108160800BIZWIRE_USPRX____BW5034&feedID=600&press_symbol=2534754|title=Emerson Fittipaldi to Become Chairman of Speedway Museum#Auto Racing Hall of Fame|Indianapolis Motor Speedway Hall of Fame]] in 2004. ==Personal life== ===Family=== [[File:Emerson Fittipaldi (Mugello, 1974).jpg|thumb|Fittipaldi with Italian fans at [[Mugello Circuit|Mugello]] in 1974]] Fittipaldi is the younger brother of former [[Formula One]] driver and team owner [[Wilson Fittipaldi Júnior|Wilson Fittipaldi]]. He is the uncle of [[International Formula 3000]] and [[IMSA SportsCar Championship|IMSA]] champion [[Christian Fittipaldi]]. He was married to Maria Helena from 1970 to 1982. They had three children. He was married a second time, to Teresa, in the mid-1980s. They have two children.{{cite web|url=https://www.newspapers.com/newspage/199707893/|title=In Step with Emerson Fittipaldi|publisher=[[The Greenville News]]|date=29 May 1994|access-date=7 February 2018}} In early December 2012, Fittipaldi married economist Rossana Fanucchi in São Paulo after a partnership of eleven years. They have a son, [[Emerson Fittipaldi Jr.|Emerson Jr.]], born in 2007, and daughter Vittoria, born in late 2010.{{cite web|url=https://globoesporte.globo.com/motor/formula-1/noticia/2012/12/emerson-fittipaldi-oficializa-uniao-com-rossana-fanucchi-em-sao-paulo.html|title=Emerson Fittipaldi oficializa união com Rossana Fanucchi em São Paulo|publisher=[[Rede Globo|GloboEsporte]]|date=8 December 2012|access-date=8 February 2016}} Emerson Jr. competed in the [[2021 F4 Danish Championship]], finishing third overall.{{Cite web|last=Gripping|first=Kim|date=4 October 2021|title=Mads Hoe blev mester - men Strømsted løb med opmærksomheden|trans-title=Mads Hoe became champion - but Strømsted ran with the attention|url=https://www.formel4.dk/mads-hoe-blev-mester-men-stroemsted-loeb-med-opmaerksomheden/|url-status=dead|access-date=27 November 2021|website=Formel 4|publisher=[[F4 Danish Championship]]|language=da-DK|archive-date=27 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211127010515/https://www.formel4.dk/mads-hoe-blev-mester-men-stroemsted-loeb-med-opmaerksomheden/}} His daughter Tatiana married racing driver [[Max Papis]]. They have two children, Marco Papis and Matteo Papis; Emerson's grandsons.{{Cite web |url= http://www.espn.com/racing/nascar/cup/columns/story?id=4398982&columnist=smith_marty |title= Smith: Papis ===Life=== In September 1997, while recovering from injuries in a crash at [[Michigan International Speedway]] a year earlier, Fittipaldi was flying his private plane across his orange tree farm in the state of [[São Paulo (state)|São Paulo]]. The plane lost power and plunged {{convert|90|m|ft}} to the ground, leaving him with serious back injuries. Though Fittipaldi had converted to Christianity the year prior, his beliefs were reinforced after the crash. Fittipaldi is a Protestant in the Presbyterian tradition.{{cite web|title=60 Seconds: Emerson Fittipaldi|url=https://metro.co.uk/2008/05/01/60-seconds-emerson-fittipaldi-113348/|website=[[Metro (British newspaper)|metro.com.uk]]|access-date=7 February 2018|date=1 May 2008}} Fittipaldi was a friend of [[the Beatles]] guitarist [[George Harrison]] and was with him shortly before Harrison died in November 2001.{{cite web |url=http://www.soundsandcolours.com/articles/brazil/george-harrison-in-brazil-and-singing-tribute-to-emerson-fittipaldi/ |title=George Harrison in Brazil and Singing Tribute to Emerson Fittipaldi |publisher=Sounds and Colours |access-date=8 February 2016 |archive-date=3 December 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131203222501/http://www.soundsandcolours.com/articles/brazil/george-harrison-in-brazil-and-singing-tribute-to-emerson-fittipaldi/ |url-status=dead }} In 2016, Fittipaldi established Fittipaldi Motors and, along with [[Pininfarina]] and [[HWA Team|HWA AG]], created his first sports car project, the [[Fittipaldi EF7]], though the car never entered production.{{cite web|title=Fittipaldi EF7 Vision Gran Turismo: F1 champ turns from racing to making|url=https://www.carmagazine.co.uk/car-news/motor-shows-events/geneva/2017/fittipaldi-ef7-vision-gran-turismo-f1-champ-turns-from-racing-to-making/|website=[[Car Magazine|https://www.carmagazine.co.uk]]|access-date=7 February 2018|date=14 April 2017}} In August 2022, Fittipaldi announced his candidacy for the [[Senate of the Republic (Italy)|Italian Senate]], representing the South American [[Overseas constituencies of the Italian Parliament|overseas constituency]], running as a member of the [[Brothers of Italy]] political party,{{cite web|url=https://uk.sports.yahoo.com/news/emerson-fittipaldi-takes-first-steps-200900509.html|title=Emerson Fittipaldi takes his first steps into the Italian political system|website=Yahoo! Sport|first=Sam|last=Cooper|date=18 August 2022|accessdate=6 September 2022}} being eventually defeated by Italo-Argentine [[Mario Borghese]] a month later in the [[2022 Italian general election|2022 Italian parliamentary elections]].{{Cite web |title=Emerson Fittipaldi perde disputa pelo Senado italiano |url=https://g1.globo.com/mundo/noticia/2022/09/26/emerson-fittipaldi-perde-disputa-pelo-senado-italiano.ghtml |access-date=2022-09-27 |website=G1 |date=26 September 2022 |language=pt-br}} ==Racing
